title: Urticaria — Acute
oneLiner: Acute itchy wheals (hives) <6 weeks; common, benign but can herald life-threatening anaphylaxis; know triggers and red flags.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 22-year-old woman presents to ED with a 2-hour history of intensely itchy, raised red welts on her trunk and limbs. She started amoxicillin for a sore throat yesterday. Her lips are slightly swollen but she has no stridor, no wheeze, and her BP is 110/70. The welts blanch on pressure; each lesion disappears within 4–6 hours, only to be replaced by new ones elsewhere. She is otherwise well.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Intensely itchy (pruritus)** = hallmark; histamine-driven
- **Raised red welts** = wheals; oedema of superficial dermis
- **Trunk/limbs, not palms/soles** = typical distribution; palms/soles suggest systemic cause (e.g., vasculitis, infection)
- **Onset after drug** = temporal clue to trigger (amoxicillin)
- **Lip swelling** = associated angioedema (deeper dermal oedema); anaphylaxis risk factor
- **No stridor, wheeze, hypotension** = reassuring; no airway or circulatory compromise (yet)
- **Lesions last <24h**, new crops appear = evanescent; distinguishes from urticarial vasculitis (>24h fixed)
- **Blanches on pressure** = vasodilation, not extravasation of RBCs (vs. purpura)

**Atypical presentations (trick you):**

1. **Urticarial vasculitis** – wheals last >24h, painful (not just itchy), leave bruising/pigmentation, may have fever or arthralgias. Often misdiagnosed as simple urticaria.
2. **Physical urticaria (e.g., cold, cholinergic)** – triggered by cold exposure or exercise; lesions look identical but appear only with specific stimulus. Cholinergic: pinpoint wheals with sweating.
3. **Angioedema without wheals** – isolated swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, or genitals; consider ACE inhibitor or hereditary angioedema (C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency). No itch, no hives.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute urticaria is a sudden outbreak of red, raised, itchy bumps (wheals) that appear and disappear within hours, caused by release of histamine and other mediators from mast cells in the skin. It is “acute” when the episode lasts less than 6 weeks.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Very common** – 15–25% of people will have at least one episode; it’s a frequent ED and GP presentation.
- **Anaphylaxis can be missed** – urticaria is often the first sign; failure to assess airway/breathing/circulation can be fatal.
- **Underlying triggers are often reversible** – identifying and avoiding the cause (drug, food, infection) prevents recurrence.
- **Pitfall: prescribing steroids without antihistamine** – unnecessary overuse of systemic corticosteroids; first-line is antihistamine.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of a hive:**

Imagine a mast cell in your dermis as a grenade. It gets activated when an allergen (e.g., amoxicillin) cross-links two IgE antibodies on its surface. Or, non-immunologically, direct mast cell degranulation (e.g., opiates, radiocontrast). The grenade “explodes”: granules release histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins.

- **Histamine** binds to H1 receptors on blood vessels → vasodilation and increased permeability → **redness** (vasodilation) and **wheal** (fluid leaks into dermis)
- **Histamine also stimulates sensory nerves** → **intense itch** (pruritus)
- **Leukotrienes** cause prolonged vasodilation and contribute to **wheal formation lasting hours**
- If the mediator release extends deeper into the subcutaneous tissue → **angioedema** (lip/eyelid swelling)
- **Why new crops appear?** Rapid mediator turnover: after a few hours, local histamine is degraded and the original wheal fades. But other mast cells in adjacent skin degranulate, creating new wheals.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern cues:**

- Blanching on pressure = vessels still compressible, no frank haemorrhage (good clue against vasculitis)
- Itch = histamine; antihistamines relieve this
- Evanescence = short-lived mediator release and local histamine breakdown
- Angioedema = deeper oedema; can involve airway mucosa → stridor (emergency)
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Example | Stem clue |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | No trigger found (~50% of acute) | “No obvious cause; resolves spontaneously in weeks” |
| **Drugs** | NSAIDs, antibiotics (penicillin, sulfa), opiates, ACE inhibitors | “Started new medication 1–14 days ago” |
| **Foods** | Shellfish, eggs, milk, nuts, strawberries | “Within minutes of eating; associated angioedema common” |
| **Infections** | Viral URI (especially in children), EBV, hepatitis | “Sore throat / fever / URTI symptoms 1–2 weeks prior” |
| **Insect stings** | Bee, wasp, fire ant | “Recent sting; may have anaphylaxis” |
| **Physical** | Cold, heat, pressure, vibration, sunlight, water, exercise | “Wheals only when exposed to trigger” |
| **Autoimmune** | Thyroid disease, lupus (chronic more common) | “Recurrent; family history; other symptoms” |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic – but always search for drugs and infections first.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
Clinical diagnosis. No specific scoring system for acute urticaria. The history (duration ≤6 weeks, evanescent wheals) is diagnostic.

**What you should do (and not do):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order? | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| **None in mild case** | – | First presentation, no red flags | Reassure; treat symptomatically |
| **Allergy testing (skin prick / specific IgE)** | Identifies IgE-mediated triggers | Suspected food/drug allergy; chronic or severe | Positive result supports cause; negative doesn’t rule out |
| **Tryptase (serum) – acute** | Elevated in mast cell degranulation | Suspect anaphylaxis | >11.4 ng/mL during episode (or rise from baseline) confirms anaphylaxis |
| **CBC, ESR, CRP** | – | If suspect infection or vasculitis | Elevated WBC/CRP → infection; elevated ESR → vasculitis |
| **Thyroid antibodies, ANA** | – | Chronic recurrent urticaria (≥6 weeks) | Association with autoimmune thyroiditis |
| **Skin biopsy** | Direct immunofluorescence | Suspect urticarial vasculitis (lesions >24h, painful) | Shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis with immune deposits |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms or rules out:**

- **Evanescent wheals (<24h) + pruritus + negative systemic signs = acute urticaria** – no test needed
- **Fixed, painful wheal >24h + residual purpura** → biopsy to rule out urticarial vasculitis
sections.Immediate (first hour):
**What + why:**

- **Assess airway, breathing, circulation** – stridor, hoarseness, hypotension? If yes → anaphylaxis protocol (IM adrenaline 0.3–0.5 mg anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5–15 min as needed; oxygen, IV fluids)
  - *Why:* Laryngeal oedema can progress rapidly; adrenaline is life-saving
- **If no anaphylaxis:**
  - **Non-sedating antihistamine** – e.g., cetirizine 10 mg PO, loratadine 10 mg PO
    - *Why:* Blocks H1 receptors → reduces itch and wheal formation within 1 hour
  - **Avoid sedating antihistamines** (e.g., diphenhydramine, chlorphenamine) except if severe pruritus preventing sleep – they cause drowsiness, impair driving, and are no more effective
  - **Consider short course prednisolone** (e.g., 30–40 mg PO for 3–5 days) ONLY if: extensive, severe, or associated with angioedema
    - *Why:* Suppresses inflammation but takes 4–6 hours to work; not first-line
sections.Ward / ED observation (24–72 hours):
- **Continue antihistamines** – cetirizine 10 mg once daily (or up to 20 mg if needed); if inadequate, switch to fexofenadine 180 mg daily (higher potency)
- **Identify trigger** – detailed history: drug exposures, foods, infections; if strong suspicion, advise avoidance
- **For angioedema without anaphylaxis** – add H2 blocker (e.g., ranitidine 150 mg BD) or leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast 10 mg daily) second-line; refer to allergy for suspected ACE inhibitor
- **Patient education** – explain evanescent nature; avoid scratching; cool compresses for itch; return if any breathing difficulty or lip/tongue swelling
sections.Long-term (if recurrent or chronic >6 we:
- **Refer to dermatology or allergy** – for skin prick testing, challenge tests, or management of physical/autoimmune causes
- **Second-line agents (specialist use only):** omalizumab (anti-IgE), cyclosporine, or hydroxychloroquine for refractory chronic urticaria (not acute)
sections.Pitfalls — What NOT to do:
- ❌ **Prescribing steroids alone without antihistamine** – steroids suppress inflammation but do not block histamine; antihistamines are first-line
- ❌ **Giving IM adrenaline for mild angioedema without airway signs** – overuse causes tachycardia, anxiety, palpitations; only give if stridor, dyspnoea, or hypotension
- ❌ **Assuming it’s allergic without considering physical triggers** – leads to unnecessary food/drug avoidance
- ❌ **Repeating testing for chronic urticaria** – in acute, no need for extensive autoimmune screen unless recurrent
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **Urticaria (Acute)** | **Urticarial Vasculitis** | **Erythema Multiforme** | **Contact Dermatitis** |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Onset** | Minutes to hours | Hours to days | Over 1–2 days | Hours to days after contact |
| **Lesion duration** | <24 hours, evanescent | >24–72 hours, fixed | >7 days, target lesions | Days to weeks |
| **Pain** | Itch only | Painful/burning | Often painful | Itch and burning |
| **Appearance** | Blanching wheals | Non-blanching, purpuric, may leave pigmentation | Target lesions (dusky centre, pale ring, red halo) | Weeping, scaling, vesicular |
| **Mucosal involvement** | Not typical | Possible | Common (oral, genital, eyes) | Only if allergen contacts mucosa |
| **Systemic symptoms** | None | Fever, arthralgia | Malaise, fever | None |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Wheals come and go; pressure blanches | Lesions persist >24h, painful, residual bruising | Target lesions; recent infection (HSV) or drug | Localised to contact area; patch test positive |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Anaphylaxis** | Rapid onset (minutes to hours); urticaria + dyspnoea, stridor, wheeze, hypotension, collapse | Urticaria + hypotension (SBP <90), hypoxia, hoarse voice | IM adrenaline immediately (0.3–0.5 mg anterolateral thigh); call 2222 / crash team; IV fluids; consider IV antihistamine (chlorphenamine 10 mg slow IV) and IV hydrocortisone (200 mg) |
| **Laryngeal oedema** | Progressive hoarseness, stridor (first inspiratory, then biphasic), drooling, hypoxia | Stridor on breathing, oxygen saturations <94%, neck swelling | Airway emergency: 100% oxygen, adrenaline nebulised (5 mg or 0.5 mg/kg) if no IM access; call anaesthetics; prepare for intubation |
| **Urticarial vasculitis (if misdiagnosed)** | Persistent painful wheals >24h, arthralgia, fever, renal involvement | Non-blanching purpura; elevated ESR, low complement; urine RBC casts | Biopsy; treat with NSAIDs (mild) or prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day; refer rheumatology |
| **Secondary infection (from scratching)** | Cellulitis: localised erythema, warmth, pus, lymphangitis | Wound in area of hives; fever, leukocytosis | Antibiotics (flucloxacillin) after swab; advise emollients and antihistamines to reduce scratching |

**Late-presentation pattern (the stem that tests “what happens if untreated”):**  
A 40-year-old man with mild acute urticaria self-medicates with nothing. After 3 days, he presents to ED with progressive lip swelling, hoarse voice, and stridor 2 days after stopping his unknown ACE inhibitor. Key learning: untreated angioedema can worsen slowly; airway intervention (surgical airway if intubation fails) is time-critical.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for acute urticaria triggers: “DIAPER”**  
- **D**rugs (antibiotics, NSAIDs, opiates)  
- **I**nfections (viral, especially in kids)  
- **A**llergens (food, insect stings)  
- **P**hysical (cold, heat, pressure, exercise)  
- **E**ndocrine (thyroid, autoimmune)  
- **R**eactions (blood products, radiocontrast)

**Mnemonic for assessing severity in urticaria (for anaphylaxis): “A-B-C-2”**  
- **A**irway: stridor, hoarseness  
- **B**reathing: wheeze, dyspnoea, hypoxia  
- **C**irculation: hypotension, tachycardia, syncope  
- **2** minutes: if any present, give adrenaline IM

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Evanescence is gold** – a wheal that lasts >24h is NOT acute urticaria until proven otherwise (think vasculitis, erythema multiforme).
2. **Dermatographism** – if you can write letters on the patient’s skin with a blunt pen, it’s physical urticaria; 5% of normal population have it; always test in clinic.
3. **Angioedema without wheals + ACE inhibitor** – suspect bradykinin-mediated; antihistamines and adrenaline don’t work; stop ACEi, give icatibant (bradykinin receptor antagonist) or C1 inhibitor concentrate if hereditary.
4. **Children with fever + urticaria** – most likely viral exanthem (e.g., roseola, EBV); no need for antibiotics or antihistamines unless severe.
5. **Food allergy causing urticaria typically within 30 minutes** – delayed urticaria (hours) after eating is less likely IgE-mediated; think of non-IgE (e.g., food additives, histamine-releasing foods like shellfish).
6. **Stress is not a cause** – but can worsen chronic urticaria by mast cell activation via CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone). Don’t tell patients it’s “all in their head.”
7. **Never give IM adrenaline for mild lip swelling** – only if airway, breathing, or circulation are compromised. Overuse causes dysrhythmias and anxiety; the dose is 0.3 mg (0.3 mL of 1:1000) in adults.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**

- Mistaking urticarial rash for drug rash (exanthem) – exanthem is confluent, red, not whealy, and often has systemic symptoms
- Prescribing antihistamines “as needed” for a patient who is having continuous crops – give fixed daily dosing
- Forgetting to stop the trigger (e.g., antibiotic course) – the urticaria will recur until the drug is stopped
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to call for help | Trigger | Threshold / Criteria |
|-----------------------|---------|----------------------|
| **Senior (Reg/SpR)** | Anaphylaxis treated, but not improving; need for specialist input | Persistent hypotension after 2 doses adrenaline; angioedema requiring IV therapy; suspected urticarial vasculitis |
| **ICU / Critical Care** | Laryngeal oedema, severe anaphylaxis with two systems involved (e.g., respiratory + cardiovascular) | Stridor, hypoxia (SpO2 <90%), SBP <90 despite fluids, GCS drop, intubation required |
| **Crash team (2222)** | Cardiac arrest, periarrest, airway compromise | Unresponsiveness, no pulse, severe bradycardia, impending respiratory arrest |

**Specific thresholds (as per UK guidelines):**

- **NEWS2 ≥5** (any parameter with a score of 3) in the context of urticaria/angioedema → escalate to senior
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (if BP low) → suspect anaphylactic shock
- **GCS <15** or **fall in GCS** → consider anaphylactic cerebral hypoperfusion → crash call
- **Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) <50% predicted** in asthmatics with urticaria → severe exacerbation; senior review

**Example escalation conversation:** “I’m calling about Mr X with acute urticaria and now hoarseness, stridor, and SpO2 90% on 15L O2. He has a history of peanut allergy and just ate a granola bar. I’ve given IM adrenaline 0.3 mg 5 minutes ago, no improvement. Can you attend? I’m also calling the crash team.”

rawMarkdown:
# Urticaria — Acute

**One-liner:** Acute itchy wheals (hives) <6 weeks; common, benign but can herald life-threatening anaphylaxis; know triggers and red flags.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 22-year-old woman presents to ED with a 2-hour history of intensely itchy, raised red welts on her trunk and limbs. She started amoxicillin for a sore throat yesterday. Her lips are slightly swollen but she has no stridor, no wheeze, and her BP is 110/70. The welts blanch on pressure; each lesion disappears within 4–6 hours, only to be replaced by new ones elsewhere. She is otherwise well.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Intensely itchy (pruritus)** = hallmark; histamine-driven
- **Raised red welts** = wheals; oedema of superficial dermis
- **Trunk/limbs, not palms/soles** = typical distribution; palms/soles suggest systemic cause (e.g., vasculitis, infection)
- **Onset after drug** = temporal clue to trigger (amoxicillin)
- **Lip swelling** = associated angioedema (deeper dermal oedema); anaphylaxis risk factor
- **No stridor, wheeze, hypotension** = reassuring; no airway or circulatory compromise (yet)
- **Lesions last <24h**, new crops appear = evanescent; distinguishes from urticarial vasculitis (>24h fixed)
- **Blanches on pressure** = vasodilation, not extravasation of RBCs (vs. purpura)

**Atypical presentations (trick you):**

1. **Urticarial vasculitis** – wheals last >24h, painful (not just itchy), leave bruising/pigmentation, may have fever or arthralgias. Often misdiagnosed as simple urticaria.
2. **Physical urticaria (e.g., cold, cholinergic)** – triggered by cold exposure or exercise; lesions look identical but appear only with specific stimulus. Cholinergic: pinpoint wheals with sweating.
3. **Angioedema without wheals** – isolated swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, or genitals; consider ACE inhibitor or hereditary angioedema (C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency). No itch, no hives.

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute urticaria is a sudden outbreak of red, raised, itchy bumps (wheals) that appear and disappear within hours, caused by release of histamine and other mediators from mast cells in the skin. It is “acute” when the episode lasts less than 6 weeks.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Very common** – 15–25% of people will have at least one episode; it’s a frequent ED and GP presentation.
- **Anaphylaxis can be missed** – urticaria is often the first sign; failure to assess airway/breathing/circulation can be fatal.
- **Underlying triggers are often reversible** – identifying and avoiding the cause (drug, food, infection) prevents recurrence.
- **Pitfall: prescribing steroids without antihistamine** – unnecessary overuse of systemic corticosteroids; first-line is antihistamine.

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of a hive:**

Imagine a mast cell in your dermis as a grenade. It gets activated when an allergen (e.g., amoxicillin) cross-links two IgE antibodies on its surface. Or, non-immunologically, direct mast cell degranulation (e.g., opiates, radiocontrast). The grenade “explodes”: granules release histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins.

- **Histamine** binds to H1 receptors on blood vessels → vasodilation and increased permeability → **redness** (vasodilation) and **wheal** (fluid leaks into dermis)
- **Histamine also stimulates sensory nerves** → **intense itch** (pruritus)
- **Leukotrienes** cause prolonged vasodilation and contribute to **wheal formation lasting hours**
- If the mediator release extends deeper into the subcutaneous tissue → **angioedema** (lip/eyelid swelling)
- **Why new crops appear?** Rapid mediator turnover: after a few hours, local histamine is degraded and the original wheal fades. But other mast cells in adjacent skin degranulate, creating new wheals.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern cues:**

- Blanching on pressure = vessels still compressible, no frank haemorrhage (good clue against vasculitis)
- Itch = histamine; antihistamines relieve this
- Evanescence = short-lived mediator release and local histamine breakdown
- Angioedema = deeper oedema; can involve airway mucosa → stridor (emergency)

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Example | Stem clue |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | No trigger found (~50% of acute) | “No obvious cause; resolves spontaneously in weeks” |
| **Drugs** | NSAIDs, antibiotics (penicillin, sulfa), opiates, ACE inhibitors | “Started new medication 1–14 days ago” |
| **Foods** | Shellfish, eggs, milk, nuts, strawberries | “Within minutes of eating; associated angioedema common” |
| **Infections** | Viral URI (especially in children), EBV, hepatitis | “Sore throat / fever / URTI symptoms 1–2 weeks prior” |
| **Insect stings** | Bee, wasp, fire ant | “Recent sting; may have anaphylaxis” |
| **Physical** | Cold, heat, pressure, vibration, sunlight, water, exercise | “Wheals only when exposed to trigger” |
| **Autoimmune** | Thyroid disease, lupus (chronic more common) | “Recurrent; family history; other symptoms” |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic – but always search for drugs and infections first.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
Clinical diagnosis. No specific scoring system for acute urticaria. The history (duration ≤6 weeks, evanescent wheals) is diagnostic.

**What you should do (and not do):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order? | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| **None in mild case** | – | First presentation, no red flags | Reassure; treat symptomatically |
| **Allergy testing (skin prick / specific IgE)** | Identifies IgE-mediated triggers | Suspected food/drug allergy; chronic or severe | Positive result supports cause; negative doesn’t rule out |
| **Tryptase (serum) – acute** | Elevated in mast cell degranulation | Suspect anaphylaxis | >11.4 ng/mL during episode (or rise from baseline) confirms anaphylaxis |
| **CBC, ESR, CRP** | – | If suspect infection or vasculitis | Elevated WBC/CRP → infection; elevated ESR → vasculitis |
| **Thyroid antibodies, ANA** | – | Chronic recurrent urticaria (≥6 weeks) | Association with autoimmune thyroiditis |
| **Skin biopsy** | Direct immunofluorescence | Suspect urticarial vasculitis (lesions >24h, painful) | Shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis with immune deposits |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms or rules out:**

- **Evanescent wheals (<24h) + pruritus + negative systemic signs = acute urticaria** – no test needed
- **Fixed, painful wheal >24h + residual purpura** → biopsy to rule out urticarial vasculitis

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

### Immediate (first hour)

**What + why:**

- **Assess airway, breathing, circulation** – stridor, hoarseness, hypotension? If yes → anaphylaxis protocol (IM adrenaline 0.3–0.5 mg anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5–15 min as needed; oxygen, IV fluids)
  - *Why:* Laryngeal oedema can progress rapidly; adrenaline is life-saving
- **If no anaphylaxis:**
  - **Non-sedating antihistamine** – e.g., cetirizine 10 mg PO, loratadine 10 mg PO
    - *Why:* Blocks H1 receptors → reduces itch and wheal formation within 1 hour
  - **Avoid sedating antihistamines** (e.g., diphenhydramine, chlorphenamine) except if severe pruritus preventing sleep – they cause drowsiness, impair driving, and are no more effective
  - **Consider short course prednisolone** (e.g., 30–40 mg PO for 3–5 days) ONLY if: extensive, severe, or associated with angioedema
    - *Why:* Suppresses inflammation but takes 4–6 hours to work; not first-line

### Ward / ED observation (24–72 hours)

- **Continue antihistamines** – cetirizine 10 mg once daily (or up to 20 mg if needed); if inadequate, switch to fexofenadine 180 mg daily (higher potency)
- **Identify trigger** – detailed history: drug exposures, foods, infections; if strong suspicion, advise avoidance
- **For angioedema without anaphylaxis** – add H2 blocker (e.g., ranitidine 150 mg BD) or leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast 10 mg daily) second-line; refer to allergy for suspected ACE inhibitor
- **Patient education** – explain evanescent nature; avoid scratching; cool compresses for itch; return if any breathing difficulty or lip/tongue swelling

### Long-term (if recurrent or chronic >6 weeks)

- **Refer to dermatology or allergy** – for skin prick testing, challenge tests, or management of physical/autoimmune causes
- **Second-line agents (specialist use only):** omalizumab (anti-IgE), cyclosporine, or hydroxychloroquine for refractory chronic urticaria (not acute)

### Pitfalls — What NOT to do

- ❌ **Prescribing steroids alone without antihistamine** – steroids suppress inflammation but do not block histamine; antihistamines are first-line
- ❌ **Giving IM adrenaline for mild angioedema without airway signs** – overuse causes tachycardia, anxiety, palpitations; only give if stridor, dyspnoea, or hypotension
- ❌ **Assuming it’s allergic without considering physical triggers** – leads to unnecessary food/drug avoidance
- ❌ **Repeating testing for chronic urticaria** – in acute, no need for extensive autoimmune screen unless recurrent

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | **Urticaria (Acute)** | **Urticarial Vasculitis** | **Erythema Multiforme** | **Contact Dermatitis** |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Onset** | Minutes to hours | Hours to days | Over 1–2 days | Hours to days after contact |
| **Lesion duration** | <24 hours, evanescent | >24–72 hours, fixed | >7 days, target lesions | Days to weeks |
| **Pain** | Itch only | Painful/burning | Often painful | Itch and burning |
| **Appearance** | Blanching wheals | Non-blanching, purpuric, may leave pigmentation | Target lesions (dusky centre, pale ring, red halo) | Weeping, scaling, vesicular |
| **Mucosal involvement** | Not typical | Possible | Common (oral, genital, eyes) | Only if allergen contacts mucosa |
| **Systemic symptoms** | None | Fever, arthralgia | Malaise, fever | None |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Wheals come and go; pressure blanches | Lesions persist >24h, painful, residual bruising | Target lesions; recent infection (HSV) or drug | Localised to contact area; patch test positive |

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Anaphylaxis** | Rapid onset (minutes to hours); urticaria + dyspnoea, stridor, wheeze, hypotension, collapse | Urticaria + hypotension (SBP <90), hypoxia, hoarse voice | IM adrenaline immediately (0.3–0.5 mg anterolateral thigh); call 2222 / crash team; IV fluids; consider IV antihistamine (chlorphenamine 10 mg slow IV) and IV hydrocortisone (200 mg) |
| **Laryngeal oedema** | Progressive hoarseness, stridor (first inspiratory, then biphasic), drooling, hypoxia | Stridor on breathing, oxygen saturations <94%, neck swelling | Airway emergency: 100% oxygen, adrenaline nebulised (5 mg or 0.5 mg/kg) if no IM access; call anaesthetics; prepare for intubation |
| **Urticarial vasculitis (if misdiagnosed)** | Persistent painful wheals >24h, arthralgia, fever, renal involvement | Non-blanching purpura; elevated ESR, low complement; urine RBC casts | Biopsy; treat with NSAIDs (mild) or prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day; refer rheumatology |
| **Secondary infection (from scratching)** | Cellulitis: localised erythema, warmth, pus, lymphangitis | Wound in area of hives; fever, leukocytosis | Antibiotics (flucloxacillin) after swab; advise emollients and antihistamines to reduce scratching |

**Late-presentation pattern (the stem that tests “what happens if untreated”):**  
A 40-year-old man with mild acute urticaria self-medicates with nothing. After 3 days, he presents to ED with progressive lip swelling, hoarse voice, and stridor 2 days after stopping his unknown ACE inhibitor. Key learning: untreated angioedema can worsen slowly; airway intervention (surgical airway if intubation fails) is time-critical.

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for acute urticaria triggers: “DIAPER”**  
- **D**rugs (antibiotics, NSAIDs, opiates)  
- **I**nfections (viral, especially in kids)  
- **A**llergens (food, insect stings)  
- **P**hysical (cold, heat, pressure, exercise)  
- **E**ndocrine (thyroid, autoimmune)  
- **R**eactions (blood products, radiocontrast)

**Mnemonic for assessing severity in urticaria (for anaphylaxis): “A-B-C-2”**  
- **A**irway: stridor, hoarseness  
- **B**reathing: wheeze, dyspnoea, hypoxia  
- **C**irculation: hypotension, tachycardia, syncope  
- **2** minutes: if any present, give adrenaline IM

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Evanescence is gold** – a wheal that lasts >24h is NOT acute urticaria until proven otherwise (think vasculitis, erythema multiforme).
2. **Dermatographism** – if you can write letters on the patient’s skin with a blunt pen, it’s physical urticaria; 5% of normal population have it; always test in clinic.
3. **Angioedema without wheals + ACE inhibitor** – suspect bradykinin-mediated; antihistamines and adrenaline don’t work; stop ACEi, give icatibant (bradykinin receptor antagonist) or C1 inhibitor concentrate if hereditary.
4. **Children with fever + urticaria** – most likely viral exanthem (e.g., roseola, EBV); no need for antibiotics or antihistamines unless severe.
5. **Food allergy causing urticaria typically within 30 minutes** – delayed urticaria (hours) after eating is less likely IgE-mediated; think of non-IgE (e.g., food additives, histamine-releasing foods like shellfish).
6. **Stress is not a cause** – but can worsen chronic urticaria by mast cell activation via CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone). Don’t tell patients it’s “all in their head.”
7. **Never give IM adrenaline for mild lip swelling** – only if airway, breathing, or circulation are compromised. Overuse causes dysrhythmias and anxiety; the dose is 0.3 mg (0.3 mL of 1:1000) in adults.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**

- Mistaking urticarial rash for drug rash (exanthem) – exanthem is confluent, red, not whealy, and often has systemic symptoms
- Prescribing antihistamines “as needed” for a patient who is having continuous crops – give fixed daily dosing
- Forgetting to stop the trigger (e.g., antibiotic course) – the urticaria will recur until the drug is stopped

## 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to call for help | Trigger | Threshold / Criteria |
|-----------------------|---------|----------------------|
| **Senior (Reg/SpR)** | Anaphylaxis treated, but not improving; need for specialist input | Persistent hypotension after 2 doses adrenaline; angioedema requiring IV therapy; suspected urticarial vasculitis |
| **ICU / Critical Care** | Laryngeal oedema, severe anaphylaxis with two systems involved (e.g., respiratory + cardiovascular) | Stridor, hypoxia (SpO2 <90%), SBP <90 despite fluids, GCS drop, intubation required |
| **Crash team (2222)** | Cardiac arrest, periarrest, airway compromise | Unresponsiveness, no pulse, severe bradycardia, impending respiratory arrest |

**Specific thresholds (as per UK guidelines):**

- **NEWS2 ≥5** (any parameter with a score of 3) in the context of urticaria/angioedema → escalate to senior
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (if BP low) → suspect anaphylactic shock
- **GCS <15** or **fall in GCS** → consider anaphylactic cerebral hypoperfusion → crash call
- **Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) <50% predicted** in asthmatics with urticaria → severe exacerbation; senior review

**Example escalation conversation:** “I’m calling about Mr X with acute urticaria and now hoarseness, stridor, and SpO2 90% on 15L O2. He has a history of peanut allergy and just ate a granola bar. I’ve given IM adrenaline 0.3 mg 5 minutes ago, no improvement. Can you attend? I’m also calling the crash team.”
